text: To the brethren, the Jews that are throughout Egypt; the brethren, the Jews that are in Jerusalem, and in the land of Judea, send health and good peace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: May God be gracious to you, and remember his covenant that he made with Abraham, and Isaac, and Jacob, his faithful servants:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And give you all a heart to worship him, and to do his will with a great heart, and a willing mind.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: May he open your heart in his law, and in his commandments, and send you peace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: May he hear your prayers, and be reconciled unto you, and never forsake you in the evil time.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And now here we are praying for you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When Demetrius reigned, in the year one hundred and sixty-nine, we Jews wrote to you in the trouble and violence that came upon us in those years, after Jason withdrew himself from the holy land, and from the kingdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They burnt the gate, and shed innocent blood: then we prayed to the Lord, and were heard, and we offered sacrifices, and fine flour, and lighted the lamps, and set forth the loaves.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And now celebrate ye the days of Scenopegia in the month of Casleu.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the year one hundred and eighty-eight, the people that is at Jerusalem, and in Judea, and the senate, and Judas, to Aristobolus, the preceptor of king Ptolemee, who is of the stock of the anointed priests, and to the Jews that are in Egypt, health and welfare.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Having been delivered by God out of great dangers, we give him great thanks, forasmuch as we have been in war with such a king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he made numbers of men swarm out of Persia, that have fought against us, and the holy city.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For when the leader himself was in Persia, and with him a very great army, he fell in the temple of Nanea, being deceived by the counsel of the priests of Nanea.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For Antiochus, with his friends, came to the place as though he would marry her, and that he might receive great sums of money under the title of a dowry.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when the priests of Nanea had set it forth, and he with a small company had entered into the compass of the temple, they shut the temple,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When Antiochus was come in: and opening a secret entrance of the temple, they cast stones and slew the leader, and them that were with him, and hewed them in pieces; and cutting off their heads, they threw them forth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Blessed be God in all things, who hath delivered up the wicked.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore, whereas we purpose to keep the purification of the temple on the five and twentieth day of the month of Casleu, we thought it necessary to signify it to you: that you also may keep the day of Scenopegia, and the day of the fire, that was given when Nehemias offered sacrifice, after the temple and the altar was built.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For when our fathers were led into Persia, the priests that then were worshippers of God, took privately the fire from the altar, and hid it in a valley where there was a deep pit without water, and there they kept it safe, so that the place was unknown to all men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But when many years had passed, and it pleased God that Nehemias should be sent by the king of Persia, he sent some of the posterity of those priests that had hid it, to seek for the fire: and as they told us, they found no fire, but thick water.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then he bade them draw it up, and bring it to him: and the priest, Nehemias, commanded the sacrifices that were laid on, to be sprinkled with the same water, both the wood, and the things that were laid upon it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when this was done, and the time came that the sun shone out, which before was in a cloud, there was a great fire kindled, so that all wondered.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all the priests made prayer, while the sacrifice was consuming, Jonathan beginning, and the rest answering.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the prayer of Nehemias was after this manner: O Lord God, Creator of all things, dreadful and strong, just and merciful, who alone art the good king,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who alone art gracious, who alone art just, and almighty, and eternal, who deliverest Israel from all evil, who didst choose the fathers, and didst sanctify them:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Receive the sacrifice for all thy people Israel, and preserve thy own portion, and sanctify it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Gather together our scattered people, deliver them that are slaves to the Gentiles, and look upon them that are despised and abhorred: that the Gentiles may know that thou art our God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Punish them that oppress us, and that treat us injuriously with pride.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Establish thy people in thy holy place, as Moses hath spoken.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the priests sung hymns till the sacrifice was consumed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when the sacrifice was consumed, Nehemias commanded the water that was left to be poured out upon the great stones.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Which being done, there was kindled a flame from them: but it was consumed by the light that shined from the altar.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when this matter became public, it was told to the king of Persia, that in the place where the priests that were led away, had hid the fire, there appeared water, with which Nehemias and they that were with him had purified the sacrifices.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king considering, and diligently examining the matter, made a temple for it, that he might prove what had happened.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he had proved it, he gave the priests many goods, and divers presents, and he took and distributed them to them with his own hand.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Nehemias called this place Nephthar, which is interpreted purification. But many call it Nephi.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now it is found in the descriptions of Jeremias, the prophet, that he commanded them that went into captivity, to take the fire, as it hath been signified, and how he gave charge to them that were carried away into captivity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And how he gave them the law, that they should not forget the commandments of the Lord, and that they should not err in their minds, seeing the idols of gold, and silver, and the ornaments of them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And with other such like speeches, he exhorted them that they would not remove the law from their heart.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It was also contained in the same writing, how the prophet, being warned by God, commanded that the tabernacle and the ark should accompany him, till he came forth to the mountain where Moses went up, and saw the inheritance of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when Jeremias came thither he found a hollow cave: and he carried in thither the tabernacle, and the ark, and the altar of incense, and so stopped the door.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then some of them that followed him, came up to mark the place: but they could not find it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when Jeremias perceived it, he blamed them, saying: The place shall be unknown, till God gather together the congregation of the people, and receive them to mercy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And then the Lord will shew these things, and the majesty of the Lord shall appear, and there shall be a cloud as it was also shewed to Moses, and he shewed it when Solomon prayed that the place might be sanctified to the great God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he treated wisdom in a magnificent manner: and like a wise man, he offered the sacrifice of the dedication, and of the finishing of the temple.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And as Moses prayed to the Lord, and fire came down from heaven, and consumed the holocaust: so Solomon also prayed, and fire came down from heaven and consumed the holocaust.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Moses said: Because the sin offering was not eaten, it was consumed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Solomon also celebrated the dedication eight days.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And these same things were set down in the memoirs, and commentaries of Nehemias: and how he made a library, and gathered together out of the countries, the books both of the prophets, and of David, and the epistles of the kings, and concerning the holy gifts.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And in like manner Judas also gathered together all such things as were lost by the war we had, and they are in our possession.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wherefore, if you want these things, send some that may fetch them to you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As we are then about to celebrate the purification, we have written unto you: and you shall do well, if you keep the same days.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And we hope that God, who hath delivered his people, and hath rendered to all the inheritance, and the kingdom, and the priesthood, and the sanctuary,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As he promised in the law, will shortly have mercy upon us, and will gather us together from every land under heaven into the holy place.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he hath delivered us out of great perils, and hath cleansed the place.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now as concerning Judas Machabeus, and his brethren, and the purification of the great temple, and the dedication of the altar:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As also the wars against Antiochus, the Illustrious, and his son, Eupator:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the manifestations that came from heaven to them, that behaved themselves manfully on the behalf of the Jews, so that, being but a few they made themselves masters of the whole country, and put to flight the barbarous multitude:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And recovered again the most renowned temple in all the world, and delivered the city, and restored the laws that were abolished, the Lord with all clemency shewing mercy to them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all such things as have been comprised in five books by Jason, of Cyrene, we have attempted to abridge in one book.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For considering the multitude of books, and the difficulty that they find that desire to undertake the narrations of histories, because of the multitude of the matter,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: We have taken care for those indeed that are willing to read, that it might be a pleasure of mind: and for the studious, that they may more easily commit to memory: and that all that read might receive profit.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And as to ourselves indeed, in undertaking this work of abridging, we have taken in hand no easy task; yea, rather a business full of watching and sweat.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But as they that prepare a feast, and seek to satisfy the will of others: for the sake of many, we willingly undergo the labour.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Leaving to the authors the exact handling of every particular, and as for ourselves, according to the plan proposed, studying to be brief.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For as the master builder of a new house must have care of the whole building: but he that taketh care to paint it, must seek out fit things for the adorning of it: so must it be judged of us.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For to collect all that is to be known, to put the discourse in order, and curiously to discuss every particular point, is the duty of the author of a history:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But to pursue brevity of speech, and to avoid nice declarations of things, is to be granted to him that maketh an abridgment.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Here then we will begin the narration: let this be enough by way of a preface: for it is a foolish thing to make a long prologue, and to be short in the story itself.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore, when the holy city was inhabited with all peace, and the laws as yet were very well kept, because of the godliness of Onias, the high priest and the hatred his soul had of evil,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It came to pass that even the kings themselves and the princes esteemed the place worthy of the highest honour, and glorified the temple with very great gifts:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So that Seleucus, king of Asia, allowed out of his revenues all the charges belonging to the ministry of the sacrifices.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But one Simon, of the tribe of Benjamin, who was appointed overseer of the temple, strove in opposition to the high priest, to bring about some unjust thing in the city.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he could not overcome Onias, he went to Apollonius, the son of Tharseas, who at that time was governor of Celesyria, and Phenicia:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And told him, that the treasury in Jerusalem was full of immense sums of money, and the common store was infinite, which did not belong to the account of the sacrifices: and that it was possible to bring all into the king’s hands.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now when Apollonius had given the king notice concerning the money that he was told of, he called for Heliodorus, who had the charge over his affairs, and sent him with commission to bring him the foresaid money.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Heliodorus forthwith began his journey, under a colour of visiting the cities of Celesyria and Phenicia, but indeed to fulfil the king’s purpose.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he was come to Jerusalem, and had been courteously received in the city by the high priest, he told him what information had been given concerning the money: and declared the cause for which he was come: and asked if these things were so indeed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the high priest told him that these were sums deposited, and provisions for the subsistence of the widows and the fatherless:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And that some part of that which wicked Simon had given intelligence of belonged to Hircanus, son of Tobias, a man of great dignity; and that the whole was four hundred talents of silver, and two hundred of gold.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But that to deceive them who had trusted to the place and temple which is honoured throughout the whole world, for the reverence and holiness of it, was a thing which could not by any means be done.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he, by reason of the orders he had received from the king, said, that by all means the money must be carried to the king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So on the day he had appointed, Heliodorus entered in to order this matter. But there was no small terror throughout the whole city.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the priests prostrated themselves before the altar in their priests’ vestments, and called upon him from heaven, who made the law concerning things given to be kept, that he would preserve them safe, for them that had deposited them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now whosoever saw the countenance of the high priest, was wounded in heart: for his face, and the changing of his colour, declared the inward sorrow of his mind.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the man was so compassed with sadness and horror of the body, that it was manifest to them that beheld him, what sorrow he had in his heart.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Others also came flocking together out of their houses, praying and making public supplication, because the place was like to come into contempt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the women, girded with haircloth about their breasts, came together in the streets. And the virgins also that were shut up, came forth, some to Onias, and some to the walls, and others looked out of the windows.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all holding up their hands towards heaven made supplication.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the expectation of the mixed multitude, and of the high priest, who was in an agony, would have moved any one to pity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And these indeed called upon almighty God, to preserve the things that had been committed to them safe and sure for those that had committed them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Heliodorus executed that which he had resolved on, himself being present in the same place with his guard about the treasury.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the spirit of the Almighty God gave a great evidence of his presence, so that all that had presumed to obey him, falling down by the power of God, were struck with fainting and dread.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For there appeared to them a horse, with a terrible rider upon him, adorned with a very rich covering: and he ran fiercely and struck Heliodorus with his fore feet, and he that sat upon him seemed to have armour of gold.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Moreover there appeared two other young men, beautiful and strong, bright and glorious, and in comely apparel: who stood by him, on either side, and scourged him without ceasing with many stripes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Heliodorus suddenly fell to the ground, and they took him up, covered with great darkness, and having put him into a litter, they carried him out.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So he that came with many servants, and all his guard, into the aforesaid treasury, was carried out, no one being able to help him, the manifest power of God being known.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he indeed, by the power of God, lay speechless, and without all hope of recovery.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But they praised the Lord, because he had glorified his place: and the temple, that a little before was full of fear and trouble, when the Almighty Lord appeared, was filled with joy and gladness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then some of the friends of Heliodorus forthwith begged of Onias, that he would call upon the Most High to grant him his life, who was ready to give up the ghost.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So the high priest, considering that the king might perhaps suspect that some mischief had been done to Heliodorus by the Jews, offered a sacrifice of health for the recovery of the man.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when the high priest was praying, the same young men in the same clothing stood by Heliodorus, and said to him: Give thanks to Onias the priest: because for his sake the Lord hath granted thee life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And thou having been scourged by God, declare unto all men the great works and the power of God. And having spoken thus, they appeared no more.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Heliodorus, after he had offered a sacrifice to God, and made great vows to him, that had granted him life, and given thanks to Onias, taking his troops with him, returned to the king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he testified to all men the works of the great God, which he had seen with his own eyes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when the king asked Heliodorus, who might be a fit man to be sent yet once more to Jerusalem, he said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thou hast any enemy, or traitor to thy kingdom, send him thither, and thou shalt receive him again scourged, if so be he escape: for there is undoubtedly in that place a certain power of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he that hath his dwelling in the heavens, is the visitor and protector of that place, and he striketh and destroyeth them that come to do evil to it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the things concerning Heliodorus, and the keeping of the treasury, fell out in this manner.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Simon, of whom we spoke before, who was the betrayer of the money, and of his country, spoke ill of Onias, as though he had incited Heliodorus to do these things, and had been the promoter of evils:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he presumed to call him a traitor to the kingdom, who provided for the city, and defended his nation, and was zealous for the law of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But when the enmities proceeded so far, that murders also were committed by some of Simon’s friends:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Onias, considering the danger of this contention, and that Apollonius, who was the governor of Celesyria, and Phenicia, was outrageous, which increased the malice of Simon, went to the king,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Not to be an accuser of his countrymen, but with a view to the common good of all the people.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he saw that, except the king took care, it was impossible that matters should be settled in peace, or that Simon would cease from his folly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But after the death of Seleucus, when Antiochus, who was called the Illustrious, had taken possession of the kingdom, Jason, the brother of Onias, ambitiously sought the high priesthood:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And went to the king, promising him three hundred and sixty talents of silver, and out of other revenues fourscore talents.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Besides this he promised also a hundred and fifty more, if he might have license to set him up a place for exercise, and a place for youth, and to entitle them that were at Jerusalem, Antiochians.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Which when the king had granted, and he had gotten the rule into his hands, forthwith he began to bring over his countrymen to the fashion of the heathens.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And abolishing those things, which had been decreed of special favour by the kings in behalf of the Jews, by the means of John, the father of that Eupolemus, who went ambassador to Rome to make amity and alliance, he disannulled the lawful ordinances of the citizens, and brought in fashions that were perverse.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he had the boldness to set up, under the very castle, a place of exercise, and to put all the choicest youths in brothel houses.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now this was not the beginning, but an increase, and progress of heathenish and foreign manners, through the abominable and unheard of wickedness of Jason, that impious wretch, and no priest.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Insomuch that the priests were not now occupied about the offices of the altar, but despising the temple and neglecting the sacrifices, hastened to be partakers of the games, and of the unlawful allowance thereof, and of the exercise of the discus.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And setting nought by the honours of their fathers, they esteemed the Grecian glories for the best:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the sake of which they incurred a dangerous contention, and followed earnestly their ordinances, and in all things they coveted to be like them, who were their enemies and murderers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For acting wickedly against the laws of God doth not pass unpunished: but this the time following will declare.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now when the game that was used every fifth year was kept at Tyre, the king being present,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The wicked Jason sent from Jerusalem sinful men, to carry three hundred didrachmas of silver for the sacrifice of Hercules; but the bearers thereof desired it might not be bestowed on the sacrifices, because it was not necessary, but might be deputed for other charges.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So the money was appointed by him that sent it to the sacrifice of Hercules: but because of them that carried it was employed for the making of galleys.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now when Apollonius, the son of Mnestheus was sent into Egypt to treat with the nobles of king Philometor, and Antiochus understood that he was wholly excluded from the affairs of the kingdom, consulting his own interest, he departed thence and came to Joppe, and from thence to Jerusalem.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Where he was received in a magnificent manner by Jason, and the city, and came in with torch lights, and with praises, and from thence he returned with his army into Phenicia.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Three years afterwards Jason sent Menelaus, brother of the aforesaid Simon, to carry money to the king, and to bring answers from him concerning certain necessary affairs.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he being recommended to the king, when he had magnified the appearance of his power, got the high priesthood for himself, by offering more than Jason by three hundred talents of silver.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So having received the king’s mandate, he returned, bringing nothing worthy of the high priesthood: but having the mind of a cruel tyrant, and the rage of a savage beast.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Jason, who had undermined his own brother, being himself undermined, was driven out a fugitive into the country of the Ammonites.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Menelaus got the principality: but as for the money he had promised to the king, he took no care, when Sostratus, the governor of the castle, called for it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For to him appertained the gathering of the taxes: wherefore they were both called before the king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Menelaus was removed from the priesthood, Lysimachus, his brother, succeeding: and Sostratus was made governor of the Cyprians.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When these things were in doing, it fell out that they of Tharsus, and Mallos, raised a sedition, because they were given for a gift to Antiochus, the king’s concubine.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The king, therefore, went in all haste to appease them, leaving Andronicus, one of his nobles, for his deputy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Menelaus supposing that he had found a convenient time, having stolen certain vessels of gold out of the temple, gave them to Andronicus, and others he had sold at Tyre, and in the neighbouring cities:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Which when Onias understood most certainly, he reproved him, keeping himself in a safe place at Antioch, beside Daphne.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Whereupon Menelaus coming to Andronicus, desired him to kill Onias. And he went to Onias, and gave him his right hand with an oath, and (though he were suspected by him) persuaded him to come forth out of the sanctuary, and immediately slew him, without any regard to justice.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For which cause not only the Jews, but also the other nations, conceived indignation, and were much grieved for the unjust murder of so great a man.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when the king was come back from the places of Cilicia, the Jews that were at Antioch, and also the Greeks, went to him: complaining of the unjust murder of Onias.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Antiochus, therefore, was grieved in his mind for Onias, and being moved to pity, shed tears, remembering the sobriety and modesty of the deceased.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And being inflamed to anger, he commanded Andronicus to be stripped of his purple, and to be led about through all the city: and that in the same place wherein he had committed the impiety against Onias, the sacrilegious wretch should be put to death, the Lord repaying him his deserved punishment.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now when many sacrileges had been committed by Lysimachus in the temple, by the counsel of Menelaus, and the rumour of it was spread abroad, the multitude gathered themselves together against Lysimachus, a great quantity of gold being already carried away.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wherefore the multitude making an insurrection, and their minds being filled with anger, Lysimachus armed about three thousand men, and began to use violence, one Tyrannus being captain, a man far gone both in age and in madness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But when they perceived the attempt of Lysimachus, some caught up stones, some strong clubs, and some threw ashes upon Lysimachus.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And many of them were wounded, and some struck down to the ground, but all were put to flight: and as for the sacrilegious fellow himself, they slew him beside the treasury.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now concerning these matters, an accusation was laid against Menelaus.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when the king was come to Tyre, three men were sent from the ancients to plead the cause before him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Menelaus being convicted, promised Ptolemee to give him much money to persuade the king to favour him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Ptolemee went to the king in a certain court where he was, as it were to cool himself, and brought him to be of another mind:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Menelaus, who was guilty of all the evil, was acquitted by him of the accusations: and those poor men, who, if they had pleaded their cause even before Scythians, should have been judged innocent, were condemned to death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thus they that prosecuted the cause for the city, and for the people, and the sacred vessels, did soon suffer unjust punishment.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wherefore even the Tyrians, being moved with indignation, were very liberal towards their burial.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And so through the covetousness of them that were in power, Menelaus continued in authority, increasing in malice to the betraying of the citizens.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: At the same time Antiochus prepared for a second journey into Egypt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it came to pass, that through the whole city of Jerusalem, for the space of forty days, there were seen horsemen running in the air, in gilded raiment, and armed with spears, like bands of soldiers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And horses set in order by ranks, running one against another, with the shakings of shields, and a multitude of men in helmets, with drawn swords, and casting of darts, and glittering of golden armour, and of harnesses of all sorts.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wherefore all men prayed that these prodigies might turn to good.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now when there was gone forth a false rumour as though Antiochus had been dead, Jason taking with him no fewer than a thousand men, suddenly assaulted the city: and though the citizens ran together to the wall, the city at length was taken, and Menelaus fled into the castle.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Jason slew his countrymen without mercy, not considering that prosperity against one’s own kindred is a very great evil, thinking they had been enemies, and not citizens, whom he conquered.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Yet he did not get the principality, but received confusion at the end, for the reward of his treachery, and fled again into the country of the Ammonites.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: At the last, having been shut up by Aretas, the king of the Arabians, in order for his destruction, flying from city to city, hated by all men, as a forsaker of the laws and execrable, as an enemy of his country and countrymen, he was thrust out into Egypt:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he that had driven many out of their country perished in a strange land, going to Lacedemon, as if for kindred sake he should have refuge there:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he that had cast out many unburied, was himself cast forth both unlamented and unburied, neither having foreign burial, nor being partaker of the sepulchre of his fathers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now when these things were done, the king suspected that the Jews would forsake the alliance: whereupon departing out of Egypt with a furious mind, he took the city by force of arms,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And commanded the soldiers to kill, and not to spare any that came in their way, and to go up into the houses to slay.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thus there was a slaughter of young and old, destruction of women and children, and killing of virgins and infants.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there were slain in the space of three whole days fourscore thousand, forty thousand were made prisoners, and as many sold.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But this was not enough, he presumed also to enter into the temple, the most holy in all the world Menelaus, that traitor to the laws, and to his country, being his guide.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And taking in his wicked hands the holy vessels, which were given by other kings and cities, for the ornament and the glory of the place, he unworthily handled and profaned them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thus Antiochus going astray in mind, did not consider that God was angry for a while, because of the sins of the inhabitants of the city: and therefore this contempt had happened to the place:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Otherwise had they not been involved in many sins, as Heliodorus, who was sent by king Seleucus to rob the treasury, so this man also, as soon as he had come, had been forthwith scourged, and put back from his presumption.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But God did not choose the people for the place’s sake, but the place for the people’s sake.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And, therefore, the place also itself was made partaker of the evils of the people: but afterwards shall communicate in the good things thereof, and as it was forsaken in the wrath of Almighty God, shall be exalted again with great glory, when the great Lord shall be reconciled.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So when Antiochus had taken away out of the temple a thousand and eight hundred talents, he went back in all haste to Antioch, thinking through pride that he might now make the land navigable, and the sea passable on foot: such was the haughtiness of his mind.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He left also governors to afflict the people: at Jerusalem, Philip, a Phrygian by birth, but in manners more barbarous than he that set him there:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And in Gazarim, Andronicus and Menelaus, who bore a more heavy hand upon the citizens than the rest.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And whereas he was set against the Jews, he sent that hateful prince, Apollonius, with an army of two and twenty thousand men, commanding him to kill all that were of perfect age, and to sell the women and the younger sort.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who, when he was come to Jerusalem, pretending peace, rested till the holy day of the sabbath: and then the Jews keeping holiday, he commanded his men to take arms.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he slew all that were come forth to see: and running through the city with armed men, he destroyed a very great multitude.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Judas Machabeus, who was the tenth, had withdrawn himself into a desert place, and there lived amongst wild beasts in the mountains with his company: and they continued feeding on herbs, that they might not be partakers of the pollution.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But not long after the king sent a certain old man of Antioch, to compel the Jews to depart from the laws of their fathers and of God:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And to defile the temple that was in Jerusalem, and to call it the temple of Jupiter Olympius: and that in Garazim of Jupiter Hospitalis, according as they were that inhabited the place.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And very bad was this invasion of evils, and grievous to all.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the temple was full of the riot and revellings of the Gentiles: and of men lying with lewd women. And women thrust themselves of their accord into the holy places, and brought in things that were not lawful.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The altar also was filled with unlawful things, which were forbidden by the laws.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And neither were the sabbaths kept, nor the solemn days of the fathers observed, neither did any man plainly profess himself to be a Jew.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But they were led by bitter constraint on the king’s birthday to the sacrifices: and when the feast of Bacchus was kept, they were compelled to go about crowned with ivy in honour of Bacchus.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there went out a decree into the neighbouring cities of the Gentiles, by the suggestion of the Ptolemeans, that they also should act in like manner against the Jews, to oblige them to sacrifice:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And whosoever would not conform themselves to the ways of the Gentiles, should be put to death: then was misery to be seen.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For two women were accused to have circumcised their children: whom, when they had openly led about through the city, with the infants hanging at their breasts, they threw down headlong from the walls.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And others that had met together in caves that were near, and were keeping the sabbath day privately, being discovered by Philip, were burnt with fire, because they made a conscience to help themselves with their hands, by reason of the religious observance of the day.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now I beseech those that shall read this book, that they be not shocked at these calamities, but that they consider the things that happened, not as being for the destruction, but for the correction of our nation.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For it is a token of great goodness, when sinners are not suffered to go on in their ways for a long time, but are presently punished.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For, not as with other nations, (whom the Lord patiently expecteth, that when the day of judgment shall come, he may punish them in the fulness of their sins:)

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Doth he also deal with us, so as to suffer our sins to come to their height, and then take vengeance on us.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And therefore he never withdraweth his mercy from us: but though he chastise his people with adversity he forsaketh them not.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But let this suffice in a few words for a warning to the readers. And now we must come to the narration.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Eleazar one of the chief of the scribes, a man advanced in years, and of a comely countenance, was pressed to open his mouth to eat swine’s flesh.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he, choosing rather a most glorious death than a hateful life, went forward voluntarily to the torment.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And considering in what manner he was to come to it, patiently bearing, he determined not to do any unlawful things for the love of life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But they that stood by, being moved with wicked pity, for the old friendship they had with the man, taking him aside, desired that flesh might be brought which it was lawful for him to eat, that he might make as if he had eaten, as the king had commanded, of the flesh of the sacrifice:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That by so doing he might be delivered from death; and for the sake of their old friendship with the man, they did him this courtesy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he began to consider the dignity of his age, and his ancient years, and the inbred honour of his grey head, and his good life and conversation from a child; and he answered without delay, according to the ordinances of the holy law made by God, saying, that he would rather be sent into the other world.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For it doth not become our age, said he, to dissemble: whereby many young persons might think that Eleazar, at the age of fourscore and ten years, was gone over to the life of the heathens:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And so they, through my dissimulation, and for a little time of a corruptible life, should be deceived, and hereby I should bring a stain and a curse upon my old age.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For though, for the present time, I should be delivered from the punishments of men, yet should I not escape the hand of the Almighty neither alive nor dead.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wherefore, by departing manfully out of this life, I shall shew myself worthy of my old age:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I shall leave an example of fortitude to young men, if with a ready mind and constancy I suffer an honourable death, for the most venerable and most holy laws. And having spoken thus, he was forthwith carried to execution.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they that led him, and had been a little before more mild, were changed to wrath for the words he had spoken, which they thought were uttered out of arrogancy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But when he was now ready to die with the stripes, he groaned: and said: O Lord, who hast the holy knowledge, thou knowest manifestly that whereas I might be delivered from death, I suffer grievous pains in body: but in soul am well content to suffer these things, because I fear thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thus did this man die, leaving not only to young men, but also to the whole nation, the memory of his death, for an example of virtue and fortitude.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It came to pass also, that seven brethren, together with their mother, were apprehended, and compelled by the king to eat swine’s flesh against the law, for which end they were tormented with whips and scourges.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But one of them, who was the eldest, said thus: What wouldst thou ask, or learn of us? we are ready to die, rather than to transgress the laws of God, received from our fathers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the king being angry, commanded fryingpans and brazen caldrons to be made hot: which forthwith being heated,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He commanded to cut out the tongue of him that had spoken first: and the skin of his head being drawn off, to chop off also the extremities of his hands and feet, the rest of his brethren and his mother looking on.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he was now maimed in all parts, he commanded him, being yet alive, to be brought to the fire, and to be fried in the fryingpan: and while he was suffering therein long torments, the rest, together with the mother, exhorted one another to die manfully,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Saying: The Lord God will look upon the truth, and will take pleasure in us, as Moses declared in the profession of the canticle; And in his servants he will take pleasure.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So when the first was dead after this manner, they brought the next to make him a mocking stock: and when they had pulled off the skin of his head with the hair, they asked him if he would eat, before he were punished throughout the whole body in every limb.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he answered in his own language, and said: I will not do it. Wherefore he also, in the next place, received the torments of the first:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he was at the last gasp, he said thus: Thou indeed, O most wicked man, destroyest us out of this present life: but the King of the world will raise us up, who die for his laws, in the resurrection of eternal life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: After him the third was made a mocking-stock, and when he was required, he quickly put forth his tongue, and courageously stretched out his hands:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And said with confidence: These I have from heaven, but for the laws of God I now despise them, because I hope to receive them again from him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So that the king, and they that were with him, wondered at the young man’s courage, because he esteemed the torments as nothing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And after he was thus dead, they tormented the fourth in the like manner.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he was now ready to die, he spoke thus: It is better, being put to death by men, to look for hope from God, to be raised up again by him; for, as to thee, thou shalt have no resurrection unto life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when they had brought the fifth, they tormented him. But he, looking upon the king,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Said: Whereas thou hast power among men though thou art corruptible, thou dost what thou wilt but think not that our nation is forsaken by God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But stay patiently a while, and thou shalt see his great power, in what manner he will torment thee and thy seed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: After him they brought the sixth, and he being ready to die, spoke thus: Be not deceived without cause: for we suffer these things for ourselves, having sinned against our God, and things worthy of admiration are done to us:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But do not think that thou shalt escape unpunished, for that thou hast attempted to fight against God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now the mother was to be admired above measure, and worthy to be remembered by good men, who beheld her seven sons slain in the space of one day, and bore it with a good courage, for the hope that she had in God:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she bravely exhorted every one of them in her own language, being filled with wisdom; and joining a man’s heart to a woman’s thought,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She said to them: I know not how you were formed in my womb; for I neither gave you breath, nor soul, nor life, neither did I frame the limbs of every one of you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the Creator of the world, that formed the nativity of man, and that found out the origin of all, he will restore to you again, in his mercy, both breath and life, as now you despise yourselves for the sake of his laws.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now Antiochus, thinking himself despised, and withal despising the voice of the upbraider, when the youngest was yet alive, did not only exhort him by words, but also assured him with an oath, that he would make him a rich and a happy man, and, if he would turn from the laws of his fathers, would take him for a friend, and furnish him with things necessary.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But when the young man was not moved with these things, the king called the mother, and counselled her to deal with the young man to save his life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he had exhorted her with many words she promised that she would counsel her son.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So bending herself towards him, mocking the cruel tyrant, she said in her own language: My son have pity upon me, that bore thee nine months in my womb, and gave thee suck three years, and nourished thee, and brought thee up unto this age.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I beseech thee, my son, look upon heaven and earth, and all that is in them, and consider that God made them out of nothing, and mankind also:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So thou shalt not fear this tormentor, but being made a worthy partner with thy brethren, receive death, that in that mercy I may receive thee again with thy brethren.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: While she was yet speaking these words, the young man said: For whom do you stay? I will not obey the commandment of the king, but the commandment of the law which was given us by Moses.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But thou that hast been the author of all mischief against the Hebrews, shalt not escape the hand of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For we suffer thus for our sins.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And though the Lord, our God, is angry with us a little while, for our chastisement and correction, yet he will be reconciled again to his servants.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But thou, O wicked, and of all men most flagitious, be not lifted up without cause with vain hopes, whilst thou art raging against his servants.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For thou hast not yet escaped the judgment of the Almighty God, who beholdeth all things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For my brethren having now undergone a short pain, are under the covenant of eternal life: but thou, by the judgment of God, shalt receive just punishment for thy pride.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But I, like my brethren, offer up my life and my body for the laws of our fathers: calling upon God to be speedily merciful to our nation, and that thou by torments and stripes mayst confess that he alone is God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But in me, and in my brethren, the wrath of the Almighty, which hath justly been brought upon all our nation, shall cease.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the king being incensed with anger, raged against him more cruelly than all the rest, taking it grievously that he was mocked.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So this man also died undefiled, wholly trusting in the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And last of all, after the sons, the mother also was consumed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But now there is enough said of the sacrifices and of the excessive cruelties.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Judas Machabeus, and they that were with him, went privately into the towns: and calling together their kinsmen and friends, and taking unto them such as continued in the Jews’ religion, they assembled six thousand men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they called upon the Lord, that he would look upon his people that was trodden down by all and would have pity on the temple, that was defiled by the wicked:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That he would have pity also upon the city that was destroyed, that was ready to be made even with the ground, and would hear the voice of the blood that cried to him:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That he would remember also the most unjust deaths of innocent children, and the blasphemies offered to his name, and would shew his indignation on this occasion.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now when Machabeus had gathered a multitude, he could not be withstood by the heathens: for the wrath of the Lord was turned into mercy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So coming unawares upon the towns and cities, he set them on fire, and taking possession of the most commodious places, he made no small slaughter of the enemies:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And especially in the nights he went upon these expeditions, and the fame of his valour was spread abroad every where.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Philip seeing that the man gained ground by little and little, and that things for the most part succeeded prosperously with him, wrote to Ptolemee, the governor of Celesyria and Phenicia, to send aid to the king’s affairs.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he with all speed sent Nicanor, the son of Patroclus, one of his special friends, giving him no fewer than twenty thousand armed men of different nations, to root out the whole race of the Jews, joining also with him Gorgias, a good soldier, and of great experience in matters of war.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Nicanor purposed to raise for the king the tribute of two thousand talents, that was to be given to the Romans, by making so much money of the captive Jews:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wherefore he sent immediately to the cities upon the sea coast, to invite men together to buy up the Jewish slaves, promising that they should have ninety slaves for one talent, not reflecting on the vengeance which was to follow him from the Almighty.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now when Judas found that Nicanor was coming, he imparted to the Jews that were with him, that the enemy was at hand.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And some of them being afraid, and distrusting the justice of God, fled away.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Others sold all that they had left, and withal besought the Lord, that he would deliver them from the wicked Nicanor, who had sold them before he came near them:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if not for their sakes, yet for the covenant that he had made with their fathers, and for the sake of his holy and glorious name that was invoked upon them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Machabeus calling together seven thousand that were with him, exhorted them not to be reconciled to the enemies, nor to fear the multitude of the enemies who came wrongfully against them, but to fight manfully:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Setting before their eyes the injury they had unjustly done the holy place, and also the injury they had done to the city, which had been shamefully abused, besides their destroying the ordinances of the fathers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For, said he, they trust in their weapons, and in their boldness: but we trust in the Almighty Lord, who at a beck can utterly destroy both them that come against us, and the whole world.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Moreover, he put them in mind also of the helps their fathers had received from God: and how, under Sennacherib, a hundred and eighty-five thousand had been destroyed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And of the battle that they had fought against the Galatians, in Babylonia; how they, being in all but six thousand, when it came to the point, and the Macedonians, their companions, were at a stand, slew a hundred and twenty thousand, because of the help they had from heaven, and for this they received many favours.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: With these words they were greatly encouraged and disposed even to die for the laws and their country.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So he appointed his brethren captains over each division of his army; Simon, and Joseph, and Jonathan, giving to each one fifteen hundred men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And after the holy book had been read to them by Esdras, and he had given them for a watchword, The help of God: himself leading the first band, he joined battle with Nicanor:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Almighty being their helper, they slew above nine thousand men: and having wounded and disabled the greater part of Nicanor’s army, they obliged them to fly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they took the money of them that came to buy them, and they pursued them on every side.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But they came back for want of time: for it was the day before the sabbath: and therefore they did not continue the pursuit.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But when they had gathered together their arms and their spoils, they kept the sabbath: blessing the Lord who had delivered them that day, distilling the beginning of mercy upon them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then after the sabbath they divided the spoils to the feeble and the orphans, and the widows, and the rest they took for themselves and their servants.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When this was done, and they had all made a common supplication, they besought the merciful Lord, to be reconciled to his servants unto the end.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Moreover, they slew above twenty thousand of them that were with Timotheus and Bacchides, who fought against them, and they made themselves masters of the high strong holds: and they divided amongst them many spoils, giving equal portions to the feeble, the fatherless, and the widows; yea, and the aged also.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when they had carefully gathered together their arms, they laid them all up in convenient places, and the residue of their spoils they carried to Jerusalem:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They slew also Philarches, who was with Timotheus, a wicked man, who had many ways afflicted the Jews.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when they kept the feast of the victory at Jerusalem, they burnt Callisthenes, that had set fire to the holy gates, who had taken refuge in a certain house, rendering to him a worthy reward for his impieties:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But as for that most wicked man, Nicanor, who had brought a thousand merchants to the sale of the Jews,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Being, through the help of the Lord, brought down by them, of whom he had made no account, laying aside his garment of glory, fleeing through the midland country, he came alone to Antioch, being rendered very unhappy by the destruction of his army.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he that had promised to levy the tribute for the Romans, by the means of the captives of Jerusalem, now professed that the Jews had God for their protector, and therefore they could not be hurt, because they followed the laws appointed by him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: At that time Antiochus returned with dishonour out of Persia.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he had entered into the city called Persepolis, and attempted to rob the temple, and to oppress the city, but the multitude running together to arms, put them to flight: and so it fell out that Antiochus being put to flight, returned with disgrace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now when he was come about Ecbatana, he received the news of what had happened to Nicanor and Timotheus.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And swelling with anger, he thought to revenge upon the Jews the injury done by them that had put him to flight. And therefore he commanded his chariot to be driven, without stopping in his journey, the judgment of heaven urging him forward, because he had spoken so proudly, that he would come to Jerusalem, and make it a common burying place of the Jews.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the Lord, the God of Israel, that seeth all things, struck him with an incurable and an invisible plague. For as soon as he had ended these words, a dreadful pain in his bowels came upon him, and bitter torments of the inner parts.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And indeed very justly, seeing he had tormented the bowels of others with many and new torments, albeit he by no means ceased from his malice.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Moreover, being filled with pride, breathing out fire in his rage against the Jews, and commanding the matter to be hastened, it happened as he was going with violence, that he fell from the chariot, so that his limbs were much pained by a grievous bruising of the body.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thus he that seemed to himself to command even the waves of the sea, being proud above the condition of man, and to weigh the heights of the mountains in a balance, now being cast down to the ground, was carried in a litter, bearing witness to the manifest power of God in himself:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So that worms swarmed out of the body of this man, and whilst he lived in sorrow and pain, his flesh fell off, and the filthiness of his smell was noisome to the army.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the man that thought a little before he could reach to the stars of heaven, no man could endure to carry, for the intolerable stench.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And by this means, being brought from his great pride, he began to come to the knowledge of himself, being admonished by the scourge of God, his pains increasing every moment.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he himself could not now abide his own stench, he spoke thus: It is just to be subject to God, and that a mortal man should not equal himself to God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then this wicked man prayed to the Lord, of whom he was not like to obtain mercy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner
