text: And the city, to which he was going in haste to lay it even with the ground, and to make it a common burying place, he now desireth to make free:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Jews, whom he said he would not account worthy to be so much as buried, but would give them up to be devoured by the birds and wild beasts, and would utterly destroy them with their children, he now promiseth to make equal with the Athenians.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The holy temple also, which before he had spoiled, he promised to adorn with goodly gifts, and to multiply the holy vessels, and to allow out of his revenues the charges pertaining to the sacrifices.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Yea also, that he would become a Jew himself, and would go through every place of the earth, and declare the power of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But his pains not ceasing, (for the just judgment of God was come upon him) despairing of life, he wrote to the Jews, in the manner of a supplication, a letter in these words:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To his very good subjects the Jews, Antiochus, king and ruler, wisheth much health, and welfare, and happiness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If you and your children are well, and if all matters go with you to your mind, we give very great thanks.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As for me, being infirm, but yet kindly remembering you, returning out of the places of Persia, and being taken with a grievous disease, I thought it necessary to take care for the common good:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Not distrusting my life, but having great hope to escape the sickness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But considering that my father also, at what time he led an army into the higher countries, appointed who should reign after him:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To the end that if any thing contrary to expectation should fall out, or any bad tidings should be brought, they that were in the countries, knowing to whom the whole government was left, might not be troubled.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Moreover, considering that neighbouring princes, and borderers, wait for opportunities, and expect what shall be the event, I have appointed my son, Antiochus, king, whom I often recommended to many of you, when I went into the higher provinces: and I have written to him what I have joined here below.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I pray you, therefore, and request of you, that, remembering favours both public and private, you will every man of you continue to be faithful to me and to my son.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For I trust that he will behave with moderation and humanity, and following my intentions, will be gracious unto you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thus the murderer and blasphemer being grievously struck, as himself had treated others, died a miserable death in a strange country, among the mountains.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Philip, that was brought up with him, carried away his body: and out of fear of the son of Antiochus, went into Egypt to Ptolemee Philometor.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Machabeus, and they that were with him, by the protection of the Lord, recovered the temple and the city again.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he threw down the altars which the heathens had set up in the streets, as also the temples of the idols.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And having purified the temple, they made another altar: and taking fire out of the fiery stones, they offered sacrifices after two years, and set forth incense, and lamps, and the loaves of proposition.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when they had done these things, they besought the Lord, lying prostrate on the ground, that they might no more fall into such evils; but if they should at any time sin, that they might be chastised by him more gently, and not be delivered up to barbarians and blasphemous men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now upon the same day that the temple had been polluted by the strangers on the very same day it was cleansed again; to wit, on the five and twentieth day of the month of Casleu.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they kept eight days with joy, after the manner of the feast of the tabernacles, remembering that not long before they had kept the feast of the tabernacles when they were in the mountains, and in dens like wild beasts.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore they now carried boughs and green branches and palms, for him that had given them good success in cleansing his place.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they ordained by a common statute, and decree, that all the nation of the Jews should keep those days every year.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And this was the end of Antiochus, that was called the Illustrious.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But now we will repeat the acts of Eupator, the son of that wicked Antiochus, abridging the account of the evils that happened in the wars.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For when he was come to the crown, he appointed over the affairs of his realm one Lysias, general of the army of Phenicia and Syria.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For Ptolemee, that was called Macer, was determined to be strictly just to the Jews and especially by reason of the wrong that had been done them, and to deal peaceably with them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But being accused for this to Eupator by his friends, and being oftentimes called traitor, because he had left Cyprus, which Philometor had committed to him, and coming over to Antiochus the Illustrious, had revolted also from him, he put an end to his life by poison.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Gorgias, who was governor of the holds, taking with him the strangers, often fought against the Jews.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Jews that occupied the most commodious holds, received those that were driven out of Jerusalem, and attempted to make war.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then they that were with Machabeus, beseeching the Lord by prayers to be their helper, made a strong attack upon the strong holds of the Idumeans:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And assaulting them with great force, won the holds, killed them that came in the way, and slew altogether no fewer than twenty thousand.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And whereas some were fled into very strong towers, having all manner of provision to sustain a siege,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Machabeus left Simon and Joseph, and Zacheus, and them that were with them, in sufficient number to besiege them, and departed to those expeditions which urged more.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now they that were with Simon, being led with covetousness, were persuaded for the sake of money by some that were in the towers: and taking seventy thousand didrachmas, let some of them escape.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But when it was told Machabeus what was done, he assembled the rulers of the people, and accused those men that they had sold their brethren for money, having let their adversaries escape.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So he put these traitors to death, and forthwith took the two towers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And having good success in arms, and all things he took in hand, he slew more than twenty thousand in the two holds.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Timotheus, who before had been overcome by the Jews, having called together a multitude of foreign troops, and assembled horsemen out of Asia, came as though he would take Judea by force of arms.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Machabeus, and they that were with him, when he drew near, prayed to the Lord, sprinkling earth upon their heads, and girding their loins with haircloth,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And lying prostrate at the foot of the altar, besought him to be merciful to them, and to be an enemy to their enemies, and an adversary to their adversaries, as the law saith.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And so after prayer taking their arms, they went forth further from the city, and when they were come very near the enemies they rested.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But as soon as the sun was risen both sides joined battle: the one part having, with their valour, the Lord for a surety of victory, and success: but the other side making their rage their leader in battle.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But when they were in the heat of the engagement, there appeared to the enemies from heaven five men upon horses, comely, with golden bridles, conducting the Jews:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Two of them took Machabeus between them, and covered him on every side with their arms, and kept him safe; but cast darts and fireballs against the enemy, so that they fell down, being both confounded with blindness, and filled with trouble.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there were slain twenty thousand five hundred, and six hundred horsemen.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Timotheus fled into Gazara, a strong hold where Chereas was governor.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Machabeus, and they that were with him cheerfully laid siege to the fortress four days.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But they that were within, trusting to the strength of the place, blasphemed exceedingly, and cast forth abominable words.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But when the fifth day appeared, twenty young men of them that were with Machabeus, inflamed in their minds, because of the blasphemy, approached manfully to the wall, and pushing forward with fierce courage, got up upon it:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Moreover, others also getting up after them, went to set fire to the towers and the gates, and to burn the blasphemers alive.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And having for two days together pillaged and sacked the fortress, they killed Timotheus, who was found hid in a certain place: they slew also his brother Chereas, and Apollophanes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when this was done, they blessed the Lord with hymns and thanksgiving, who had done great things in Israel, and given them the victory.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A short time after this Lysias, the king’s lieutenant, and cousin, and who had chief charge over all the affairs, being greatly displeased with what had happened,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Gathered together fourscore thousand men, and all the horsemen, and came against the Jews, thinking to take the city, and make it a habitation of the Gentiles:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And to make a gain of the temple, as of the other temples of the Gentiles and to set the high priesthood to sale every year:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Never considering the power of God, but puffed up in mind, and trusting in the multitude of his foot soldiers, and the thousands of his horsemen, and his fourscore elephants.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So he came into Judea, and approaching to Bethsura, which was in a narrow place, the space of five furlongs from Jerusalem, he laid siege to that fortress.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But when Machabeus, and they that were with him, understood that the strong holds were besieged, they and all the people besought the Lord with lamentations and tears, that he would send a good angel to save Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Machabeus himself first taking his arms, exhorted the rest to expose themselves together with him, to the danger, and to succour their brethren.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when they were going forth together with a willing mind, there appeared at Jerusalem a horseman going before them in white clothing, with golden armour, shaking a spear.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then they all together blessed the merciful Lord, and took great courage: being ready to break through not only men, but also the fiercest beasts, and walls of iron.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So they went on courageously, having a helper from heaven, and the Lord, who shewed mercy to them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And rushing violently upon the enemy, like lions, they slew of them eleven thousand footmen, and one thousand six hundred horsemen:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And put all the rest to flight; and many of them being wounded, escaped naked: Yea, and Lysias himself fled away shamefully, and escaped.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And as he was a man of understanding, considering with himself the loss he had suffered, and perceiving that the Hebrews could not be overcome, because they relied upon the help of the Almighty God, he sent to them:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And promised that he would agree to all things that are just, and that he would persuade the king to be their friend.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Machabeus consented to the request of Lysias, providing for the common good in all things; and whatsoever Machabeus wrote to Lysias, concerning the Jews, the king allowed of.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For there were letters written to the Jews from Lysias, to this effect: Lysias, to the people of the Jews, greeting.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: John, and Abesalom, who were sent from you, delivering your writings, requested that I would accomplish those things which were signified by them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore whatsoever things could be reported to the king, I have represented to him: and he hath granted as much as the matter permitted.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If, therefore, you will keep yourselves loyal in affairs, hereafter also I will endeavour to be a means of your good.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But as concerning other particulars, I have given orders by word both to these, and to them that are sent by me, to commune with you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Fare ye well. In the year one hundred and forty-eight, the four and twentieth day of the month of Dioscorus.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the king’s letter contained these words King Antiochus to Lysias, his brother, greeting.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Our father being translated amongst the gods we are desirous that they that are in our realm should live quietly, and apply themselves diligently to their own concerns.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And we have heard that the Jews would not consent to my father to turn to the rites of the Greeks but that they would keep to their own manner of living and therefore that they request us to allow them to live after their own laws.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wherefore being desirous that this nation also should be at rest, we have ordained and decreed, that the temple should be restored to them, and that they may live according to the custom of their ancestors.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou shalt do well, therefore, to send to them, and grant them peace, that our pleasure being known, they may be of good comfort, and look to their own affairs.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the king’s letter to the Jews was in this manner: King Antiochus to the senate of the Jews, and to the rest of the Jews, greeting.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If you are well, you are as we desire: we ourselves also are well.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Menelaus came to us, saying that you desired to come down to your countrymen, that are with us.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: We grant, therefore, a safe conduct to all that come and go, until the thirtieth day of the month of Xanthicus,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That the Jews may use their own kind of meats, and their own laws, as before: and that none of them any manner of ways be molested for things which have been done by ignorance.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And we have sent also Menelaus to speak to you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Fare ye well. In the year one hundred and forty-eight, the fifteenth day of the month of Xanthicus.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The Romans also sent them a letter, to this effect: Quintus Memmius, and Titus Manilius, ambassadors of the Romans, to the people of the Jews, greeting.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Whatsoever Lysias, the king’s cousin, hath granted to you, we also have granted.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But touching such things as he thought should be referred to the king, after you have diligently conferred among yourselves, send some one forthwith, that we may decree as it is convenient for you: for we are going to Antioch.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And therefore make haste to write back, that we may know of what mind you are.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Fare ye well. In the year one hundred and forty-eight, the fifteenth day of the month of Xanthicus.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When these covenants were made, Lysias went to the king, and the Jews gave themselves to husbandry.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But they that were behind, namely, Timotheus, and Apollonius, the son of Genneus, also Hieronymus, and Demophon, and besides them Nicanor, the governor of Cyprus, would not suffer them to live in peace, and to be quiet.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The men of Joppe also were guilty of this kind of wickedness: they desired the Jews, who dwelt among them, to go with their wives and children into the boats, which they had prepared, as though they had no enmity to them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Which when they had consented to, according to the common decree of the city, suspecting nothing, because of the peace: when they were gone forth into the deep, they drowned no fewer than two hundred of them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But as soon as Judas heard of this cruelty done to his countrymen, he commanded the men that were with him: and after having called upon God, the just judge,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He came against those murderers of his brethren, and set the haven on fire in the night, burnt the boats, and slew with the sword them that escaped from the fire.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he had done these things in this manner, he departed as if he would return again, and root out all the Joppites.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But when he understood that the men of Jamnia also designed to do in like manner to the Jews that dwelt among them,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He came upon the Jamnites also by night, and set the haven on fire, with the ships, so that the light of the fire was seen at Jerusalem, two hundred and forty furlongs off.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when they were now gone from thence nine furlongs, and were marching towards Timotheus, five thousand footmen, and five hundred horsemen of the Arabians, set upon them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And after a hard fight, in which, by the help of God, they got the victory, the rest of the Arabians being overcome, besought Judas for peace, promising to give him pastures, and to assist him in other things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Judas thinking that they might be profitable indeed in many things, promised them peace, and after having joined hands, they departed to their tents.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He also laid siege to a certain strong city, encompassed with bridges and walls, and inhabited by multitudes of different nations, the name of which is Casphin.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But they that were within it, trusting in the strength of the walls, and the provision of victuals, behaved in a more negligent manner, and provoked Judas with railing and blaspheming, and uttering such words as were not to be spoken.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Machabeus calling upon the great Lord of the world, who without any rams or engines of war threw down the walls of Jericho, in the time of Josue, fiercely assaulted the walls.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And having taken the city by the will of the Lord, he made an unspeakable slaughter, so that a pool adjoining, of two furlongs broad, seemed to run with the blood of the slain.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: From thence they departed seven hundred and fifty furlongs, and came to Characa, to the Jews that are called Tubianites.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But as for Timotheus, they found him not in those places, for before he had dispatched any thing he went back, having left a very strong garrison in a certain hold:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Dositheus, and Sosipater, who were captains with Machabeus, slew them that were left by Timotheus in the hold, to the number of ten thousand men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Machabeus having set in order about him six thousand men, and divided them by bands, went forth against Timotheus, who had with him a hundred and twenty thousand footmen, and two thousand five hundred horsemen.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now when Timotheus had knowledge of the coming of Judas, he sent the women and children, and the other baggage, before him into a fortress, called Carnion: for it was impregnable, and hard to come at, by reason of the straitness of the places.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But when the first band of Judas came in sight, the enemies were struck with fear, by the presence of God, who seeth all things, and they were put to flight one from another, so that they were often thrown down by their own companions, and wounded with the strokes of their own swords.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Judas was vehemently earnest in punishing the profane, of whom he slew thirty thousand men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Timotheus himself fell into the hands of the band of Dositheus and Sosipater, and with many prayers he besought them to let him go with his life, because he had the parents and brethren of many of the Jews, who, by his death, might happen to be deceived.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he had given his faith that he would restore them according to the agreement, they let him go without hurt, for the saving of their brethren.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Judas went away to Carnion, where he slew five and twenty thousand persons.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And after he had put to flight and destroyed these, he removed his army to Ephron, a strong city, wherein there dwelt a multitude of divers nations: and stout young men standing upon the walls, made a vigorous resistance: and in this place there were many engines of war, and a provision of darts.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But when they had invocated the Almighty, who with his power breaketh the strength of the enemies, they took the city: and slew five and twenty thousand of them that were within.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: From thence they departed to Scythopolis, which lieth six hundred furlongs from Jerusalem.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the Jews that were among the Scythopolitans testifying that they were used kindly by them, and that even in the times of their adversity they had treated them with humanity:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They gave them thanks, exhorting them to be still friendly to their nation, and so they came to Jerusalem, the feast of the weeks being at hand.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And after Pentecost they marched against Gorgias, the governor of Idumea.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he came out with three thousand footmen and four hundred horsemen.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when they had joined battle, it happened that a few of the Jews were slain.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Dositheus, a horseman, one of Bacenor’s band, a valiant man, took hold of Gorgias: and when he would have taken him alive, a certain horseman of the Thracians came upon him, and cut off his shoulder: and so Gorgias escaped to Maresa.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But when they that were with Esdrin had fought long, and were weary, Judas called upon the Lord to be their helper, and leader of the battle:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then beginning in his own language, and singing hymns with a loud voice, he put Gorgias’s soldiers to flight.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Judas having gathered together his army, came into the city Odollam: and when the seventh day came, they purified themselves according to the custom, and kept the sabbath in the same place.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the day following Judas came with his company, to take away the bodies of them that were slain, and to bury them with their kinsmen, in the sepulchres of their fathers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they found under the coats of the slain, some of the donaries of the idols of Jamnia, which the law forbiddeth to the Jews: so that all plainly saw, that for this cause they were slain.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then they all blessed the just judgment of the Lord, who had discovered the things that were hidden.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And so betaking themselves to prayers, they besought him, that the sin which had been committed might be forgotten. But the most valiant Judas exhorted the people to keep themselves from sin, forasmuch as they saw before their eyes what had happened, because of the sins of those that were slain.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And making a gathering, he sent twelve thousand drachms of silver to Jerusalem for sacrifice to be offered for the sins of the dead, thinking well and religiously concerning the resurrection.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: (For if he had not hoped that they that were slain should rise again, it would have seemed superfluous and vain to pray for the dead,)

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And because he considered that they who had fallen asleep with godliness, had great grace laid up for them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It is therefore a holy and wholesome thought to pray for the dead, that they may be loosed from sins.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the year one hundred and forty-nine, Judas understood that Antiochus Eupator was coming with a multitude against Judea,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And with him Lysias, the regent, who had charge over the affairs of the realm, having with him a hundred and ten thousand footmen, five thousand horsemen, twenty-two elephants, and three hundred chariots armed with hooks.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Menelaus also joined himself with them: and with great deceitfulness besought Antiochus, not for the welfare of his country, but in hopes that he should be appointed chief ruler.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the King of kings stirred up the mind of Antiochus against the sinner, and upon Lysias suggesting that he was the cause of all the evils, he commanded (as the custom is with them) that he should be apprehended and put to death in the same place.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now there was in that place a tower fifty cubits high, having a heap of ashes on every side: this had a prospect steep down.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: From thence he commanded the sacrilegious wretch to be thrown down into the ashes, all men thrusting him forward unto death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And by such a law it happened that Menelaus the transgressor of the law, was put to death: not having so much as burial in the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And indeed very justly, for insomuch as he had committed many sins against the altar of God, the fire and ashes of which were holy: he was condemned to die in ashes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the king, with his mind full of rage, came on to shew himself worse to the Jews than his father was.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Which when Judas understood, he commanded the people to call upon the Lord day and night, that as he had always done, so now also he would help them:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Because they were afraid to be deprived of the law, and of their country, and of the holy temple: and that he would not suffer the people, that had of late taken breath for a little while, to be again in subjection to blasphemous nations.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So when they had all done this together, and had craved mercy of the Lord with weeping and fasting, lying prostrate on the ground for three days continually, Judas exhorted them to make themselves ready.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he, with the ancients, determined before the king should bring his army into Judea, and make himself master of the city, to go out, and to commit the event of the thing to the judgment of the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So committing all to God, the Creator of the world, and having exhorted his people to fight manfully, and to stand up even to death for the laws, the temple, the city, their country, and citizens: he placed his army about Modin.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And having given his company for a watchword, The victory of God, with most valiant chosen young men, he set upon the king’s quarter by night, and slew four thousand men in the camp, and the greatest of the elephants, with them that had been upon him,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And having filled the camp of the enemies with exceeding great fear and tumult, they went off with good success.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now this was done at the break of day, by the protection and help of the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the king having taken a taste of the hardiness of the Jews, attempted to take the strong places by policy:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he marched with his army to Bethsura, which was a strong hold of the Jews: but he was repulsed, he failed, he lost his men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now Judas sent necessaries to them that were within

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Rhodocus, one of the Jews’ army, disclosed the secrets to the enemies, so he was sought out, and taken up, and put in prison.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Again the king treated with them that were in Bethsura: gave his right hand: took theirs: and went away.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He fought with Judas: and was overcome. And when he understood that Philip, who had been left over the affairs, had rebelled at Antioch, he was in a consternation of mind, and entreating the Jews, and yielding to them, he swore to all things that seemed reasonable, and, being reconciled, offered sacrifice, honoured the temple, and left gifts.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He embraced Machabeus, and made him governor and prince from Ptolemais unto the Gerrenians.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But when he was come to Ptolemais, the men of that city were much displeased with the conditions of the peace, being angry for fear they should break the covenant.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Lysias went up to the judgment seat, and set forth the reason, and appeased the people, and returned to Antioch: and thus matters went with regard to the king’s coming and his return.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But after the space of three years Judas, and they that were with him, understood that Demetrius, the son of Seleucus, was come up with a great power, and a navy by the haven of Tripolis, to places proper for his purpose,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And had made himself master of the countries against Antiochus, and his general, Lysias.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now one Alcimus, who had been chief priest, but had wilfully defiled himself in the time of mingling with the heathens, seeing that there was no safety for him, nor access to the altar,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Came to king Demetrius in the year one hundred and fifty, presenting unto him a crown of gold, and a palm, and besides these, some boughs that seemed to belong to the temple. And that day indeed he held his peace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But having gotten a convenient time to further his madness, being called to counsel by Demetrius, and asked what the Jews relied upon, and what were their counsels,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He answered thereunto: They among the Jews that are called Assideans, of whom Judas Machabeus is captain, nourish wars, and raise seditions, and will not suffer the realm to be in peace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For I also being deprived of my ancestors’ glory (I mean of the high priesthood) am now come hither:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Principally indeed out of fidelity to the king’s interests, but in the next place also to provide for the good of my countrymen: for all our nation suffereth much from the evil proceedings of these men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wherefore, O king, seeing thou knowest all these things, take care, I beseech thee, both of the country, and of our nation, according to thy humanity which is known to all men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For as long as Judas liveth it is not possible that the state should be quiet.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now when this man had spoken to this effect the rest also of the king’s friends, who were enemies of Judas, incensed Demetrius against him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And forthwith he sent Nicanor, the commander over the elephants, governor into Judea:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Giving him in charge, to take Judas himself: and disperse all them that were with him, and to make Alcimus the high priest of the great temple.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the Gentiles who had fled out of Judea, from Judas, came to Nicanor by flocks, thinking the miseries and calamities of the Jews to be the welfare of their affairs.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now when the Jews heard of Nicanor’s coming, and that the nations were assembled against them, they cast earth upon their heads, and made supplication to him who chose his people to keep them for ever, and who protected his portion by evident signs.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then at the commandment of their captain, they forthwith removed from the place where they were, and went to the town of Dessau, to meet them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now Simon, the brother of Judas, had joined battle with Nicanor: but was frightened with the sudden coming of the adversaries.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Nevertheless Nicanor hearing of the valour of Judas’s companions, and the greatness of courage, with which they fought for their country, was afraid to try the matter by the sword.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wherefore he sent Posidonius, and Theodotius and Matthias before to present and receive the right hands.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when there had been a consultation thereupon, and the captain had acquainted the multitude with it, they were all of one mind to consent to covenants.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So they appointed a day upon which they might come together by themselves: and seats were brought out, and set for each one.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Judas ordered armed men to be ready in convenient places, lest some mischief might be suddenly practised by the enemies: so they made an agreeable conference.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Nicanor abode in Jerusalem, and did no wrong, but sent away the flocks of the multitudes that had been gathered together.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Judas was always dear to him from the heart, and he was well affected to the man.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he desired him to marry a wife, and to have children. So he married: he lived quietly, and they lived in common.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Alcimus seeing the love they had one to another, and the covenants, came to Demetrius, and told him that Nicanor had assented to the foreign interest, for that he meant to make Judas, who was a traitor to the kingdom, his successor.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the king, being in a rage, and provoked with this man’s wicked accusations, wrote to Nicanor, signifying that he was greatly displeased with the covenant of friendship: and that he commanded him nevertheless to send Machabeus prisoner in all haste to Antioch.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When this was known, Nicanor was in a consternation, and took it grievously that he should make void the articles that were agreed upon, having received no injury from the man.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But because he could not oppose the king, he watched an opportunity to comply with the orders.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But when Machabeus perceived that Nicanor was more stern to him, and that when they met together as usual he behaved himself in a rough manner; and was sensible that this rough behaviour came not of good, he gathered together a few of his men, and hid himself from Nicanor.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he finding himself notably prevented by the man, came to the great and holy temple: and commanded the priests that were offering the accustomed sacrifices, to deliver him the man.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when they swore unto him, that they knew not where the man was whom he sought, he stretched out his hand to the temple,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And swore, saying: Unless you deliver Judas prisoner to me, I will lay this temple of God even with the ground, and will beat down the altar, and I will dedicate this temple to Bacchus.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he had spoken thus, he departed. But the priests stretching forth their hands to heaven, called upon him that was ever the defender of their nation, saying in this manner:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou, O Lord of all things, who wantest nothing, wast pleased that the temple of thy habitation should be amongst us.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore now, O Lord, the holy of all holies, keep this house for ever undefiled, which was lately cleansed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now Razias, one of the ancients of Jerusalem, was accused to Nicanor, a man that was a lover of the city, and of good report, who for his kindness was called the father of the Jews.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: This man, for a long time, had held fast his purpose of keeping himself pure in the Jews’ religion, and was ready to expose his body and life, that he might persevere therein.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Nicanor being willing to declare the hatred that he bore the Jews, sent five hundred soldiers to take him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he thought by ensnaring him to hurt the Jews very much.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now as the multitude sought to rush into his house, and to break open the door, and to set fire to it, when he was ready to be taken, he struck himself with his sword:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Choosing to die nobly rather than to fall into the hands of the wicked, and to suffer abuses unbecoming his noble birth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But whereas through haste he missed of giving a sure wound, and the crowd was breaking into the doors, he ran boldly to the wall, and manfully threw himself down to the crowd:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But they quickly making room for his fall, he came upon the midst of the neck.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And as he had yet breath in him, being inflamed in mind, he arose: and while his blood ran down with a great stream, and he was grievously wounded, he ran through the crowd:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And standing upon a steep rock, when he was now almost without blood, grasping his bowels, with both hands he cast them upon the throng, calling upon the Lord of life and spirit, to restore these to him again: and so he departed this life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But when Nicanor understood that Judas was in the places of Samaria, he purposed to set upon him with all violence, on the sabbath day.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when the Jews that were constrained to follow him, said: Do not act so fiercely and barbarously, but give honour to the day that is sanctified: and reverence him that beholdeth all things:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That unhappy man asked, if there were a mighty One in heaven, that had commanded the sabbath day to be kept.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when they answered: There is the living Lord himself in heaven, the mighty One, that commanded the seventh day to be kept.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then he said: And I am mighty upon the earth, and I command to take arms, and to do the king’s business. Nevertheless he prevailed not to accomplish his design.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Nicanor being puffed up with exceeding great pride, thought to set up a public monument of his victory over Judas.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Machabeus ever trusted with all hope that God would help them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he exhorted his people not to fear the coming of the nations, but to remember the help they had before received from heaven, and now to hope for victory from the Almighty.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And speaking to them out of the law, and the prophets, and withal putting them in mind of the battles they had fought before, he made them more cheerful:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then after he had encouraged them, he shewed withal the falsehood of the Gentiles, and their breach of oaths.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So he armed every one of them, not with defence of shield and spear, but with very good speeches, and exhortations, and told them a dream worthy to be believed, whereby he rejoiced them all.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now the vision was in this manner. Onias, who had been high priest, a good and virtuous man, modest in his looks, gentle in his manners, and graceful in speech, and who from a child was exercised in virtues holding up his hands, prayed for all the people of the Jews:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: After this there appeared also another man, admirable for age, and glory, and environed with great beauty and majesty:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Onias answering, said: This is a lover of his brethren, and of the people of Israel: this is he that prayeth much for the people, and for all the holy city, Jeremias, the prophet of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Whereupon Jeremias stretched forth his right hand, and gave to Judas a sword of gold, saying:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Take this holy sword, a gift from God, wherewith thou shalt overthrow the adversaries of my people Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thus being exhorted with the words of Judas, which were very good, and proper to stir up the courage, and strengthen the hearts of the young men, they resolved to fight, and to set upon them manfully: that valour might decide the matter, because the holy city, and the temple were in danger.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For their concern was less for their wives, and children, and for their brethren, and kinsfolks: but their greatest and principal fear was for the holiness of the temple.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they also that were in the city, had no little concern for them that were to be engaged in battle.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And now when all expected what judgment would be given, and the enemies were at hand, and the army was set in array, the beasts and the horsemen ranged in convenient places,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Machabeus considering the coming of the multitude, and the divers preparations of armour, and the fierceness of the beasts, stretching out his hands to heaven, called upon the Lord, that worketh wonders, who giveth victory to them that are worthy, not according to the power of their arms, but according as it seemeth good to him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And in his prayer he said after this manner: Thou, O Lord, who didst send thy angel in the time of Ezechias, king of Juda, and didst kill a hundred and eighty-five thousand of the army of Sennacherib:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Send now also, O Lord of heaven, thy good angel before us, for the fear and dread of the greatness of thy arm,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That they may be afraid, who come with blasphemy against thy holy people. And thus he concluded his prayer.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Nicanor, and they that were with him came forward, with trumpets and songs.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Judas, and they that were with him, encountered them, calling upon God by prayers:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So fighting with their hands, but praying to the Lord with their hearts, they slew no less than five and thirty thousand, being greatly cheered with the presence of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when the battle was over, and they were returning with joy, they understood that Nicanor was slain in his armour.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then making a shout, and a great noise, they blessed the Almighty Lord in their own language.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Judas, who was altogether ready, in body and mind, to die for his countrymen, commanded that Nicanor’s head, and his hand, with the shoulder, should be cut off, and carried to Jerusalem.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he was come thither, having called together his countrymen, and the priests to the altar, he sent also for them that were in the castle,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And shewing them the head of Nicanor, and the wicked hand, which he had stretched out, with proud boasts, against the holy house of the Almighty God,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He commanded also, that the tongue of the wicked Nicanor should be cut out, and given by pieces to birds, and the hand of the furious man to be hanged up over against the temple.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then all blessed the Lord of heaven, saying: Blessed be he that hath kept his own place undefiled.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he hung up Nicanor’s head in the top of the castle, that it might be an evident and manifest sign of the help of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they all ordained by a common decree, by no means to let this day pass without solemnity:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But to celebrate the thirteenth day of the month of Adar, called in the Syrian language, the day before Mardochias’ day.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So these things being done with relation to Nicanor, and from that time the city being possessed by the Hebrews, I also will here make an end of my narration.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Which if I have done well, and as it becometh the history, it is what I desired: but if not so perfectly, it must be pardoned me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For as it is hurtful to drink always wine, or always water, but pleasant to use sometimes the one, and sometimes the other: so if the speech be always nicely framed, it will not be grateful to the readers. But here it shall be ended.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner
